raw_case:
Asymptomatic pulmonary cusps aneurysm in newborn
Pulmonary sinus aneurysm (PSA) is an abnormal dilatation of the sinus of the pulmonary valve. This condition is exceedingly rare. A few reports of pulmonary artery aneurysms and just 2 reports of pulmonary sinus aneurysms have been reported in the literature. Various factors contribute to pulmonary artery aneurysms, including pulmonary valve stenosis, pulmonary hypertension, and other diseases that induce arterial wall debilitation, such as connective tissue disorders, vasculitis, rheumatologic maladies, and infections. It can present with some symptoms linked with other congenital anomalies or remain asymptomatic. Recently, we encountered a case of asymptomatic pulmonary sinus aneurysm in neonatal. Echocardiography showed that the aneurysm of the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve mimicked tumors at the pulmonary root.

Introduction

Pulmonary sinus aneurysm (PSA) is a rare lesion with abnormal dilatation of the sinus of the pulmonary valve. It can result from a range of contributing factors. We presented a case of asymptomatic congenital pulmonary sinus aneurysm in neonatal, which was identified as the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve.

Case report

We present the case of an asymptomatic pulmonary sinus aneurysm in a full-term newborn boy. The family medical history did not disclose any congenital disorders. In pregnancy, the baby was diagnosed with a pulmonary root mass at 25 weeks of gestation without right ventricular outflow tract obstruction. The pregnancy progressed uneventfully; the baby was born at 39 weeks and 2 days gestation. Physical examination findings were unremarkable at birth, with the neonate displaying normal vital signs, no dysmorphic features, or respiratory distress. Cardiac auscultation revealed regular heart sounds without murmurs. Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with an intermediate axis. Subsequently, echocardiography was indicated to confirm the mass on the pulmonary root.Transthoracic echocardiography showed situs solitus and levocardia with atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial concordance. Aneurysm of the pulmonary sinus with the normal pulmonary artery and branches was diagnosed. Two outpouchings of the 2 cusps of the pulmonary valve measured 6-8 mm (Fig. 1). The aneurysm cusps bulge toward the right ventricular outflow tract, identified as the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve. There was no pulmonary valve stenosis or right ventricular outflow tract obstruction (Video 1). There was mild pulmonary valve regurgitation (Video 2).Video 1 in 2D and Video 3 in 3D reconstruction showed a parasternal short-axis view with 2 sacculars at the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve. A parasternal long-axis view along the pulmonary artery (Fig. 1, Video 3) showed a dilatation of the anterior cusps toward the right infundibulum, and there was no coaptation between the anterior cusp and the left cusp, leading to mild pulmonary regurgitation (Video 4). The pulmonary trunk and 2 branches were normal in size (Video 4).The dilatation of the pulmonary sinus was identified as the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve (Fig. 2).Conservative treatment was chosen because there was no symptom, no stenosis, or marked regurgitation of the pulmonary valve. With a risk for pulmonary obstruction and thrombus, we decided to schedule follow-up every 3 to 6 months with an endocarditis prevention strategy. We used TTE to serially examine the pulmonary valve, leaflet size, and the right ventricular outflow tract. We repeated echocardiography at 3 and 6 months; there was no change in the pulmonary cusps. The right and anterior cusps are still dilated with 2 sacs at the root, and there was no obstruction. The most recent TTE showed trivial pulmonary regurgitation, good biventricular function.

Discussion

Unlike the relatively more common occurrence of aortic sinus of Valsalva aneurysms, isolated pulmonary cusp aneurysms represent a rare and unconventional clinical entity, often coexisting with other congenital anomalies. The term "pulmonary sinus aneurysm" has been sparingly documented in a few scientific literature [[1], [2], [3]] Common causes of pulmonary aneurysms are congenital and present from birth. There are also acquired causes development later in life, such as chest trauma or infective endocarditis.Various etiologies contribute to pulmonary artery aneurysms, including pulmonary valve stenosis, pulmonary hypertension, and a spectrum of disorders predisposing to arterial wall weakening, including connective tissue diseases, vasculitis, rheumatologic disorders, and infections. Rare causative factors encompass systemic hypertension, atherosclerosis, cystic medial necrosis, and traumatic injury. Page [4] said that pathogenesis could be similar to pulmonary arterial aneurysms. However, a paper needs to be more detailed about the etiopathogenesis of the pulmonary sinus aneurysm. In our presented case, the presence of this aneurysm was noted during pregnancy, reinforcing its congenital origin. It may be caused by connective tissue diseases. Because of no symptoms and the parent did not consent to test, we have not tested for genetic abnormalities yet.The very first case in literature was an incidental autopsy finding in a 7-year-old white boy. The isolated aneurysm of the pulmonary anterior cusp valve revealed no inflammatory reaction. The lesion is believed to be due to a congenital defect [5]. The clinical presentation may mirror symptoms of concurrent congenital defects or manifest asymptomatically. Because of its rarity, little is known about the management of pulmonary sinus aneurysms.Congenital anomalies of the pulmonary valve are very rare and associated with progressive valvular regurgitation and/or stenosis. The pulmonary valve, between the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery, plays a crucial role in regulating blood flow from the heart to the lungs. An aneurysm in one of its cusps can disrupt the valve function, potentially leading to complications such as valve regurgitation or obstruction of blood flow. Kanna et al. [1] reported a case of right ventricular outflow tract obstruction with an 8-month-old male infant presented with lower respiratory tract infection and failure to thrive. That patient had a ventricular septal defect, and both the sinuses of the anterior pulmonary leaflets had oval defects, which formed tiny blind sacs. These sacs were lined with fibrous tissue, and the bulk of this tissue was protruding below the valve, leading to the right ventricle outflow tract obstruction. These defects were evaluated to be the mouths of the aneurysmal sinuses of Valsalva, distinct from the leaflet tissue that was thin and pliable. In our case, the anterior and right cusps was dilated, and we can see the abnormal anterior cusps coaptation between the anterior cusp and left cusp, leading to mild pulmonary regurgitation.The possible clinical presentations of pulmonary cusp may vary accordingly, depending on their size, site, and structure [1]. Gajjar et al., [6] reported a case of pulmonary cusp aneurysm with significant ventricular septel defect and patent ductus arteriosus, had dilated pulmonary artery with an aneurysm of the anterior sinus of Valsalva. Our case was noted from the fetus, and the lesion was small and had no symptoms in the 6-month follow-up. This case needs observation for progressive valvular regurgitation and/or stenosis or risk of rupture.Diagnosis of a pulmonary cusp aneurysm often involves imaging studies such as echocardiography, cardiac computed tomography angiography, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging to visualize the structure and assess its impact on cardiac function and right ventricular outflow tract. Treatment depends on the severity of the condition and the presence of symptoms. Close monitoring may be sufficient in some cases, while others may require surgical intervention to repair or replace the affected valve. Echocardiography is the first-line diagnostic imaging method, which is significant and convenient in detecting and monitoring the pulmonary cusp aneurysm. Different imaging modalities, such as cardiac computed tomography, angiography, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, could have supplied more extensive information about this defect, mainly when symptoms occur.Since pulmonary cusp aneurysms are relatively rare, ongoing research is being conducted to better understand their underlying causes, risk factors, and optimal management strategies. Early detection and appropriate treatment are essential for minimizing the risk of complications and improving outcomes for individuals with this condition.

Conclusion

Pulmonary sinus aneurysm is extremely rare and represents a rare yet clinically significant entity that demands careful consideration in both diagnostic and therapeutic approaches. Through this case, we have underscored the diverse etiologies, including congenital defects and connective tissue disorders, contributing to the development of pulmonary cusp aneurysms at neonate. Notably, echocardiography has facilitated early detection and characterization of these lesions, enabling timely intervention and management.

CARE checklist (2016) statement

Open-AccessCountry/Territory of originVietnamAvailability of data and materialsData and materials used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Ethics approval and consent to participate

Our institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases or case series.

Consent for publication

Not applicable.

Patient consent

I confirm that that written, informed consent for publication of their case was obtained from the patient's mother, allowing us to use the patient's photographs and medical information in this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** A full-term newborn male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Asymptomatic lesion noted during prenatal evaluation.
- **History of Present Illness:** None.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Full-term birth at 39 weeks and 2 days with no complications during pregnancy.
- **Family History:** No congenital disorders.
- **Physical Examination:** Normal vital signs, no dysmorphic features, regular heart sounds without murmurs, and no respiratory distress.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Electrocardiography:** Sinus rhythm with intermediate axis.
  - **Prenatal Imaging:** Mass identified on the pulmonary root during pregnancy at 25 weeks gestation.
  - **Echocardiography:**
    - Situs solitus, levocardia, and normal atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial connections.
    - Aneurysm of anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve, measuring 6–8 mm.
    - No pulmonary valve stenosis or right ventricular outflow tract obstruction.
    - Mild pulmonary regurgitation was noted.
    - The pulmonary trunk and branches were normal in size.
    - Follow-up imaging at 3 and 6 months showed no changes in the aneurysm or significant progression.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential List:**
   - Congenital pulmonary sinus aneurysm (PSA).
   - Pulmonary valve stenosis.
   - Right ventricular outflow tract obstruction.
   - Connective tissue or congenital disorders affecting the pulmonary valve.
   - Infective causes or vasculitis-induced lesions.

2. **Evaluation of Differential List:**
   - Pulmonary valve stenosis and right ventricular outflow tract obstruction were ruled out as echocardiography showed no obstruction.
   - Infective causes or vasculitis unlikely due to the prenatal origin and absence of clinical symptoms or inflammation.
   - Connective tissue disorders could not be fully excluded, but there was no genetic testing performed, and family history was negative.
   - The lesion was consistent with a congenital pulmonary sinus aneurysm based on echocardiographic findings, showing aneurysmal dilation of the anterior and right pulmonary valve cusps without associated functional disturbances such as significant regurgitation or stenosis.

3. **Rationale for Diagnosis:**
   - The diagnosis of congenital PSA was supported by imaging findings, the asymptomatic nature of the presentation, and the prenatal detection of the lesion. No other significant congenital anomalies or systemic disorders were identified.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Congenital pulmonary sinus aneurysm involving the anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve with mild pulmonary regurgitation and no obstruction. 

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Identify the primary finding:** The key finding from the case summary is the "aneurysm of anterior and right cusps of the pulmonary valve" detected by echocardiography, which was initially noted as a mass during prenatal imaging.

<step 2> **Correlate with clinical presentation:** The patient is a newborn male, asymptomatic, and without any significant past medical or family history of congenital disorders. This suggests a congenital and likely isolated anomaly.

<step 3> **Analyze echocardiography details:** The echocardiogram provides further specific information: the aneurysm size (6-8 mm), location (anterior and right cusps), absence of pulmonary valve stenosis or RVOT obstruction, mild pulmonary regurgitation, and normal pulmonary arteries. These details are consistent with a diagnosis of pulmonary valve aneurysm and indicate it's not causing significant hemodynamic compromise.

<step 4> **Consider follow-up imaging results:** The fact that follow-up imaging at 3 and 6 months showed no change in the aneurysm size or significant progression further supports a stable and likely benign condition, typical for many congenital pulmonary valve aneurysms.

<step 5> **Formulate the most likely diagnosis:** Based on the prenatal detection, echocardiographic confirmation of a pulmonary valve cusp aneurysm, absence of significant hemodynamic consequences, and stable follow-up, the most probable diagnosis is a congenital pulmonary valve aneurysm.

<step 6> **Refine the diagnosis for precision:**  Since the aneurysm is specifically located on the pulmonary valve and is congenital in nature (detected prenatally and present in a newborn), the most accurate and concise diagnosis is "Pulmonary valve aneurysm".  Given the context of a newborn and prenatal detection, the congenital nature is implicit, but explicitly stating "Congenital pulmonary valve aneurysm" is also acceptable for increased clarity.  However, for a concise final diagnosis result, "Pulmonary valve aneurysm" is sufficient and directly addresses the observed pathology.

### Answer:
Pulmonary valve aneurysm
